heuristics.rule: Do not disclose visa/sponsorship status before the company has decided they want you
heuristics.condition: When applying or in early interviews as an international student requiring sponsorship...
heuristics.action: ...avoid proactively mentioning OPT/H-1B status unless explicitly asked; research sponsorship capacity through H-1B data and alumni intelligence instead
heuristics.because: Sponsorship cost becomes an elimination filter before your qualifications are weighed — disclosing early raises costs before benefits are established

heuristics.rule: Negotiate salary using market data, never personal need or visa-adjusted pricing
heuristics.condition: When countering an offer as a visa holder...
heuristics.action: ...ground the counter in levels.fyi, H-1B salary data, and peer compensation ranges rather than personal financial needs or immigration constraints
heuristics.because: Disclosing your reservation price or framing requests around personal need reveals your floor and eliminates negotiating room

heuristics.rule: A higher salary negotiation is simultaneously a better H-1B lottery outcome post-2025
heuristics.condition: When negotiating compensation under the wage-weighted H-1B lottery system...
heuristics.action: ...explicitly frame targeting a Level III or IV salary as benefiting both parties — higher comp for you, better lottery odds for the company's investment
heuristics.because: Salary and immigration interests are directly aligned under wage-weighted lottery rules, making this a rare non-zero-sum negotiation argument

heuristics.rule: Reframe visa dependency as a retention differentiator, not a cost
heuristics.condition: When an employer treats sponsorship as a burden during comp discussions...
heuristics.action: ...explicitly state that H-1B holders stay 2–3 years minimum due to switching complexity, making them a long-term retention asset that justifies the salary requested
heuristics.because: This flips the power dynamic from 'I'm a cost because of my visa' to 'I'm a retention asset,' neutralizing the employer's implicit leverage

heuristics.rule: Verify sponsorship execution capacity before signing, not just sponsorship willingness
heuristics.condition: When a company says they will sponsor H-1B but you have no confirmation...
heuristics.action: ...ask explicitly: do they have immigration counsel, have they sponsored before, what is their approval rate, who is currently on H-1B there, and get written confirmation before accepting
heuristics.because: Sponsorship willingness and execution capacity are different risks — a company without immigration infrastructure is a material risk regardless of intent

heuristics.rule: Sponsorship cost does not justify below-market comp
heuristics.condition: When an employer implies your salary should be lower because of sponsorship overhead...
heuristics.action: ...counter that sponsorship attorney and USCIS fees total $3,000–$8,000/year, which is less than 5% of a $150,000+ employee's total cost — not a justification for 20% below-market pay
heuristics.because: The actual cost of sponsorship is small relative to total compensation; treating it as a salary discount is a negotiation tactic, not a financial reality

heuristics.rule: Know H-1B portability rights to make competing threats credible
heuristics.condition: When feeling trapped at a current employer due to visa dependency...
heuristics.action: ...internalize that H-1B portability allows switching employers without waiting for approval if the new petition is filed within 60 days, making the threat to leave or imply alternatives genuinely credible
heuristics.because: Perceived lock-in is the source of employer leverage; knowing your legal rights converts a structural disadvantage into a negotiable constraint

heuristics.rule: Never name a salary number first — the first number anchors the entire negotiation
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter or HR form asks you to state your salary expectations or current compensation
heuristics.action: Deflect with 'I'm more focused on fit right now — if we're right for each other, we can be flexible on numbers' or enter $1 on forms to proceed
heuristics.because: Whoever names first sets the ceiling for their own outcome; if you name low, the company accepts and you leave money on the table; if you name high without knowing their range, you may anchor awkwardly

heuristics.rule: Only negotiate compensation after the company has reached 'Yes-If' status — not while still in 'No-But' evaluation
heuristics.condition: When you are still in interview or evaluation stages and a recruiter tries to discuss compensation
heuristics.action: Defer with 'I look forward to receiving your offer' — signal that comp discussion comes after the hiring decision, not during it
heuristics.because: Negotiating while the company is still deciding whether to hire you forces excessive compromise; once they've decided they want you, leverage shifts substantially in your favor

heuristics.rule: Treat negotiation as a fixed rule, not a situational choice — always counter at least once
heuristics.condition: When any job offer arrives, regardless of how generous it seems
heuristics.action: Counter or request at least one adjustment before accepting
heuristics.because: The worst-case outcome of negotiating is receiving the original offer unchanged; upside exists, downside does not — making it a pure expected-value positive action every time

heuristics.rule: When salary hits a ceiling, pivot immediately to signing bonus or equity
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter signals that base salary cannot move due to pay bands or HR constraints
heuristics.action: Say 'If you could increase the signing bonus to X, I could accept this salary' — reframe the ask to a one-time cost
heuristics.because: Companies have asymmetric budget flexibility; signing bonuses don't affect ongoing payroll equity or pay-band precedent, making them easier for employers to grant while giving you equivalent real value

heuristics.rule: Proclaim a specific reason for every negotiation request to dramatically increase acceptance rate
heuristics.condition: When making any counter-request on salary, bonus, equity, or other terms
heuristics.action: Attach a concrete justification — student loans, relocation costs, market data — even if the reason is modest
heuristics.because: Reasons make requests feel human and legitimate rather than greedy; humans respond to narrative context, and justified requests are psychologically easier for recruiters to approve internally

heuristics.rule: Explicitly call out exploding offer deadlines — never let them silently expire
heuristics.condition: When an offer comes with a 24–72 hour expiration window
heuristics.action: Directly state 'This timeline doesn't work for me — I need time to make an informed decision and consult with my family'
heuristics.because: Virtually every exploding offer extends when called out explicitly; but silently assuming they'll still want you after expiry is a mistake — the extension only happens if you request it

heuristics.rule: Use an external decision-maker buffer to deflect immediate pressure
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter is pushing for a same-call decision or immediate commitment
heuristics.action: Say 'I'll review this and discuss with my family — I'll follow up if I have questions'
heuristics.because: Removes you from direct real-time pressure; the recruiter cannot demand an instant decision if the decision is not solely yours, buying you time without appearing uninterested

heuristics.rule: Reveal a quantified value proposition during negotiation to reframe the cost of your salary request
heuristics.condition: When you have a prior achievement that maps directly to the company's core business metric
heuristics.action: Quantify your past impact ('I increased sales 3% at my last company — what would 1% mean here?') and let them calculate before discussing your ask
heuristics.because: Once the employer anchors on a large value figure (e.g., millions in revenue), a $4,000–$10,000 salary increase becomes psychologically trivial by comparison

heuristics.rule: Contact other companies immediately when you receive a strong offer to compress timelines
heuristics.condition: When you receive an offer from one company while other processes are still ongoing
heuristics.action: Email other companies: 'I've received a strong offer and my timeline is compressed — can you expedite?'
heuristics.because: This is not manipulation — it naturally surfaces genuine interest from other parties and creates real competing leverage; companies respond rationally to competitive signals

heuristics.rule: Maintain visible enthusiasm throughout the negotiation, even when numbers disappoint
heuristics.condition: When an offer comes in lower than expected or the company pushes back on your counter
heuristics.action: Reiterate excitement for the mission and desire to make it work before restating your ask
heuristics.because: Companies fear candidates who 'go bad' during negotiations; if your enthusiasm visibly drops, you become less attractive at exactly the moment you need the most leverage

heuristics.rule: Never name a number or give a yes/no on the first offer call
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter presents an offer and expects a response on the call
heuristics.action: Ask clarifying questions about level, vesting, bonus target, and equity cliff, then request a few days to consider before reconnecting
heuristics.because: You lose negotiating leverage the moment you accept or anchor a number; delay preserves all options and signals deliberateness

heuristics.rule: Value early-stage startup equity at $0 for financial planning purposes
heuristics.condition: When evaluating a compensation package from a Seed or Series A company
heuristics.action: Base all financial modeling on cash salary only and treat equity as a lottery ticket
heuristics.because: 80-90% of startups fail and liquidity may never materialize; planning around equity claims leads to real financial miscalculation

heuristics.rule: Move to signing bonus or equity refresh when base salary is capped
heuristics.condition: When a recruiter says the base salary cannot be increased further
heuristics.action: Explicitly ask whether signing bonus or equity refresh is possible, framing these as alternatives to base
heuristics.because: Signing bonuses don't affect recurring payroll budgets and equity refreshes can often be approved outside normal comp bands, making them easier for managers to approve

heuristics.rule: Use the final commitment close only once and only when genuinely ready to sign
heuristics.condition: When you have reached your target total comp number and are ready to commit
heuristics.action: Say 'If you can get to [X total comp], I'll sign today' as a binding commitment, not a negotiating probe
heuristics.because: This technique only works if it is credible; using it prematurely or insincerely destroys trust and eliminates its leverage effect

heuristics.rule: Anchor counter-offers to objective third-party data rather than personal need
heuristics.condition: When forced to name a target compensation figure during negotiation
heuristics.action: Reference levels.fyi percentile data for the specific level and role at comparable companies as the basis for your range
heuristics.because: Anchoring to market data depersonalizes the ask and makes it harder for recruiters to dismiss; it frames your number as industry-standard rather than arbitrary

heuristics.rule: Start processes with slow-moving large companies first, layer in fast-moving startups later
heuristics.condition: When running multiple interview processes simultaneously to generate competing offers
heuristics.action: Initiate Google, Amazon, Microsoft processes first (3-6 weeks), then start startup processes (1-2 weeks) so timelines converge
heuristics.because: Simultaneous offers maximize negotiation leverage; misaligned timelines force you to accept or decline before you have competitive data

heuristics.rule: Always ask for the offer level, not just the total comp number
heuristics.condition: When evaluating an offer from a company with leveled compensation bands
heuristics.action: Confirm which band level the offer is placed at and whether it is at the low, mid, or high end of that band
heuristics.because: A low end of a senior band beats a high end of a mid band for long-term career progression and future earning potential

heuristics.rule: Negotiate over phone or video, not email or text alone
heuristics.condition: When preparing to counter an offer
heuristics.action: Request a call rather than sending a written counter-offer
heuristics.because: Calls allow the recruiter to become an advocate who can 'fight for you' internally; text-only negotiation reduces the recruiter to a passive message relay

decision_factors.factor: Company's H-1B sponsorship history and approval rate
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A company with no prior H-1B sponsorships or low approval rates represents a material risk that the offer is not executable, regardless of the salary number
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check public USCIS H-1B disclosure data, ask directly whether they have immigration counsel and current H-1B employees, and request confirmation in writing before signing

decision_factors.factor: OEWS wage level classification of the offer
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Post-2025, wage level directly determines H-1B lottery odds — Level III or IV gives significantly better odds, meaning the wage level affects both comp and immigration outcomes simultaneously
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Cross-reference the offered salary against OEWS wage tables for the job's SOC code and geographic location to determine which level it falls into

decision_factors.factor: OPT expiration date and H-1B filing deadline
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Timeline urgency changes negotiation posture — a student with 6 months of OPT remaining has more flexibility than one with 6 weeks, affecting BATNA credibility and process parallelism
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate exact OPT end date, STEM OPT extension eligibility, and the H-1B cap filing window (April 1 each year) to map your actual runway

decision_factors.factor: Quality and credibility of BATNA
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Without a real alternative — whether a competing offer, another degree program, CPT position, or another country's job market — counter-offers lack credibility and the employer retains leverage
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Identify at least one path you would genuinely pursue if this offer fell through; a BATNA only works if you would actually use it

decision_factors.factor: Whether the company has decided they want you before visa status is disclosed
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Leverage shifts dramatically once a company is invested in hiring you — sponsorship cost is weighed against losing a desired candidate, not against an unknown applicant
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Track whether you've received a verbal or written signal of intent to hire before any sponsorship conversation begins

decision_factors.factor: Market compensation data for the role and level
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Negotiating from objective market data removes the need to disclose personal need and establishes an external benchmark that is harder to dismiss than personal requests
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Use levels.fyi, H-1B salary disclosure data for the specific company and role, and peer data to establish a credible range before negotiating

decision_factors.factor: Whether the student is negotiating from fear or from data
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Fear-based negotiation leads to accepting the first offer and systematically under-negotiating by 10–30% compared to domestic peers, compounding to hundreds of thousands of dollars over a career
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask yourself: is my counter grounded in a salary range from public data, or am I asking for what feels safe given my visa anxiety?

decision_factors.factor: Whether immigration commitment is confirmed in writing
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Verbal sponsorship promises are unenforceable; without written confirmation, the offer may be real on comp but not real on immigration
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Request explicit written confirmation of H-1B filing intent as part of the offer package before signing

decision_factors.factor: Whether you have competing offers from companies you'd actually work for
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A real competing offer is the single most reliable source of negotiating leverage — it transforms your ask from opinion into market evidence and creates genuine optionality
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Count active offers or imminent offers from companies you would genuinely accept; distinguish real competing offers from vague 'other conversations'

decision_factors.factor: Timing overlap of multiple active offer windows
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Leverage from competing offers only exists if offer windows actually overlap; a competing offer that expired two weeks ago provides no real pressure
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Map interview start dates and typical decision timelines; start large-company interviews first (slower, wider windows) and startup interviews later (faster, tighter) to maximize overlap

decision_factors.factor: Whether the company has reached 'Yes-If' vs. 'No-But' status on hiring you
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Negotiating before the company has decided to hire you forces compromise; negotiating after they've committed gives you full leverage
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether you've received a formal or informal offer signal; 'we'd like to make you an offer' is Yes-If; 'we're still evaluating' is No-But

decision_factors.factor: Information asymmetry — what the company knows about your current salary and alternatives
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Your negotiating power is directly proportional to what the company does not know about your floor and alternatives; revealing your current salary or reservation price collapses that power
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Audit what you've disclosed: current comp, specific asks, how you rank this offer vs. others — each disclosure reduces asymmetry

decision_factors.factor: Which compensation dimensions have flexibility at this specific company
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Companies have asymmetric flexibility across comp components; salary may be locked by pay bands while signing bonuses, equity, and vacation are freely adjustable
decision_factors.how_to_assess: When salary hits resistance, ask 'What can you approve on your own?' to identify which levers remain open

decision_factors.factor: How differentiated your skills are relative to this company's core needs
decision_factors.why_it_matters: You are more valuable to companies where your specific skills are scarce and central; differentiation yields stronger offers and more negotiating room
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask 'What are the key projects defining this company's future, and how does someone get on those teams?' — assess fit between your strengths and their stated priorities

decision_factors.factor: How much the company has already invested in your interview process
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Sunk interview costs create strong pressure on the company to reach agreement; a company that has spent tens of thousands evaluating you is highly motivated not to lose you over modest salary differences
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Count interview rounds completed, number of interviewers involved, time elapsed — more investment means more pressure on their side

decision_factors.factor: The long-term compounding effect of the current negotiation outcome
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Each offer sets the baseline for future raises and the anchor for your next employer's offer; undercompensation compounds across decades
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate the 10-year gross value of a $5K salary difference; assess whether this role sets a comp baseline you want to carry forward

decision_factors.factor: Whether justifications for requests are market-based vs. personal-need-based
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Market data ('comparable roles pay X') is far more persuasive than personal circumstance ('I have high rent'); the type of justification affects acceptance rate
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Before negotiating, gather external compensation benchmarks (Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, Blind, peer offers) to anchor requests in market reality rather than personal need

decision_factors.factor: Candidate's visible enthusiasm and likelihood-to-sign signal
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Companies invest more negotiating effort in candidates they believe they can close; enthusiasm is itself a form of leverage when it signals genuine interest without desperation
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether your communication throughout the process has conveyed excitement about the specific company and role, not just about compensation

decision_factors.factor: Company stage and equity liquidity timeline
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Equity is 30-60% of total comp at large tech companies but may be worth $0 at early-stage companies; misvaluing this reverses offer rankings
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask for the 409A valuation, preferred share liquidation preferences, and estimated IPO or liquidity timeline; apply a discount rate based on stage (public=80-90%, Series C-D=60-90%, Seed/A=0%)

decision_factors.factor: Option exercise window after leaving
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A 90-day exercise window means you must pay to exercise vested options within 90 days of leaving or forfeit them; a 10-year window gives you flexibility to wait for a liquidity event
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask directly: 'How long after leaving do I have to exercise my options?'; standard is 90 days, best-in-class is 5-10 years

decision_factors.factor: Vesting schedule structure
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A 4-year cliff (all equity vests at year 4) versus quarterly vesting after a 1-year cliff is a massive practical difference in when you can access the value
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask explicitly for the vesting schedule; confirm whether there is a cliff, the cliff duration, and the post-cliff cadence (monthly vs. quarterly)

decision_factors.factor: Presence and quality of a competing offer
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A competing offer from a company you would genuinely work for is the single most reliable lever for increasing any offer
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether you have an offer from a credible competitor at a comparable or higher total comp; never fabricate; evaluate your genuine willingness to accept it

decision_factors.factor: Total compensation versus base salary
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Two offers at the same base can differ by $100,000+ annually once equity, bonus, and signing bonus are included; comparing by base produces wrong rankings
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Build a full annual comp model: annualized equity (grant divided by vesting years), target bonus percentage, and base salary; treat signing bonus separately as non-recurring

decision_factors.factor: Signing bonus recurrence and clawback terms
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Signing bonuses are one-time; modeling them as ongoing comp inflates perceived annual earnings; clawback provisions can require repayment if you leave early
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask whether the signing bonus is subject to clawback and over what period; model annual comp without it

decision_factors.factor: Fully diluted ownership percentage
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The percentage of the company your equity represents matters more than the raw number of shares; future dilution from funding rounds affects actual value
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask: 'What percentage of the company does this grant represent on a fully diluted basis?'; compare against 409A valuation to understand dollar value

decision_factors.factor: Negotiation style: competitive firmness combined with collaborative warmth
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Research cited shows best negotiators combine firm outcome orientation with warm interpersonal approach; either alone is less effective
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether your counter-offer framing expresses genuine enthusiasm for the role while being specific and firm about the compensation target

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting the first offer to avoid friction and preserve the sponsorship relationship
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Systematically under-negotiating by 10–30% relative to domestic peers, which compounds over a career to hundreds of thousands of dollars in lost compensation
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students on OPT and early H-1B holders who conflate sponsorship preservation with salary acceptance

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Disclosing visa status early in the application process to be transparent
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Sponsorship cost becomes a filter that eliminates you before your qualifications are assessed — you lose the evaluation before the competition begins
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who interpret transparency as an early-stage obligation rather than a strategic timing decision

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Treating H-1B dependency as a permanent lock-in that prevents job switching
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Employers who understand that you believe you cannot leave will price your comp accordingly — the psychological trap becomes a real financial one even if the legal constraint is softer than believed
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: H-1B holders who don't know about the 60-day portability rule and therefore cannot credibly imply alternatives

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting a sponsorship commitment without verifying execution infrastructure
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You may sign and onboard only to discover the company has no immigration counsel, has never completed an H-1B, and cannot actually deliver the sponsorship — leaving you in status limbo
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who distinguish sponsorship willingness from execution capacity too late — after accepting the offer

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Framing long-term visa commitment as a weakness during negotiation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You surrender a genuine retention differentiator — the fact that you are likely to stay 2–3 years longer than domestic peers — and allow the employer to use it as leverage instead of you using it as value
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who have internalized the visa constraint as purely negative and haven't been exposed to the reframe

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Running a single-company job search to preserve OPT time
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You eliminate competing offer leverage entirely, capping your negotiating ceiling at whatever the single company is willing to offer without external market pressure
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: OPT students who are risk-averse about timeline and underestimate the financial return of a multi-process search

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Revealing your current salary when asked
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The company can now anchor your offer to your past underpayment rather than your market value; you've effectively justified a below-market offer by providing evidence that you've accepted below-market pay before
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who are currently underpaid relative to market, early-career professionals, and those switching from lower-paying industries or geographies

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting the first offer immediately without countering
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You signal that you either didn't know negotiation was possible or didn't believe you had standing to negotiate — which experienced hiring managers interpret as a competence signal about your professional self-awareness
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: First-time job seekers and engineers who have internalized the cultural norm that discussing compensation is unseemly

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Negotiating only on base salary
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You leave significant value on the table in dimensions where companies have far more flexibility — signing bonuses, equity, vacation, professional development, remote work — while fighting hardest on the dimension most constrained by HR pay bands
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone whose salary ask hits a hard company ceiling but who doesn't know to redirect to other levers

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Letting an exploding offer deadline pass without explicitly challenging it
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The offer lapses; the assumption that 'they'll still want me' is almost always wrong because the company interprets silence as disinterest or indecisiveness, not as a negotiating move
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who are conflict-averse and assume the company will proactively extend without being asked

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Making a specific salary counter-request early in negotiation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You reveal your floor — the company now knows the minimum you'll accept and can offer just above it rather than their true ceiling
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who mistake specificity for confidence; particularly harms those who would have received higher offers if they had maintained information asymmetry

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Treating negotiation as a one-shot transaction focused only on the current offer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You optimize for the immediate package while ignoring that the number sets your raise baseline and your next employer's anchor; short-term framing systematically undervalues the compounding effect of early-career negotiations
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Early-career professionals who are focused on getting the offer rather than optimizing lifetime earnings

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Using personal financial need as the primary justification for a higher salary
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The employer is not buying your expenses — they are buying your labor; need-based justifications are weak because they don't connect to your value, and they can create perception of financial desperation that weakens your leverage
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates with genuine financial pressure (student loans, relocation costs, family obligations) who frame it as their primary argument rather than as a supporting detail alongside market data

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Fabricating or exaggerating a competing offer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Recruiters have access to detailed compensation benchmarks and professional networks; they can often detect implausible offers, and being caught in dishonesty permanently damages the relationship and can rescind the real offer
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates with no real competing offers who feel pressure to manufacture leverage

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting a high signing bonus as part of the offer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The signing bonus inflates the perceived total comp, masking a below-market annual salary; once you model year 2 comp without it, the offer may be substantially weaker than alternatives
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Early-career candidates who are cash-constrained and anchor emotionally on the large one-time number

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Taking a startup offer with significant equity upside
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Options with a 90-day exercise window create a golden handcuff: leaving the company means either paying out-of-pocket to exercise vested options (often tens of thousands of dollars) or forfeiting them entirely
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates with limited liquid savings who cannot afford to exercise options when leaving, effectively trapping them regardless of job satisfaction

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Negotiating via email instead of phone to feel less confrontational
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The recruiter cannot advocate internally on your behalf in real time; email reduces them to a passive relay and removes the social dynamic that makes recruiters want to 'fight for you'
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Introverted or conflict-averse candidates who default to written communication as a comfort mechanism

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Reneging on an accepted offer after signing
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: While legal consequences are minimal, reputation damage within the industry is real and persistent, particularly in small or tight-knit sectors where recruiters and hiring managers move between companies
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates in niche technical domains or geographic markets where professional networks are dense and interconnected

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Using levels.fyi data without specifying level in the negotiation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Being vague about comp targets while citing market data allows the recruiter to assume a lower level benchmark; specificity about level is required for the data to support your ask
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who research market rates but fail to confirm which level their offer corresponds to

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Prioritizing base salary in negotiation when base is capped
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Spending political capital pushing against a fixed base leaves signing bonus and equity refresh untouched; these alternative levers are often approvable without escalation but are never offered proactively
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: First-time negotiators who treat base salary as the only meaningful dimension of compensation

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting the first offer out of fear that any counter will cost the sponsorship
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Visa dependency creates asymmetric loss aversion — the perceived downside of losing sponsorship feels catastrophic, overriding the upside calculus of negotiating; fear is calibrated to the wrong level of risk
failure_patterns.consequence: 10–30% systematic under-compensation relative to domestic peers; compounded over a career, this is hundreds of thousands of dollars in foregone earnings
warning_signs: Thinking 'I should just be grateful they're willing to sponsor me'
Accepting without asking any questions about comp flexibility

failure_patterns.pattern: Disclosing visa status before the company is invested in hiring you
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate honesty with timing — believing transparency requires early disclosure, or they fear being 'caught' later if they wait
failure_patterns.consequence: Sponsorship cost becomes an elimination filter before qualifications are weighed, reducing interview opportunities unnecessarily
warning_signs: Mentioning OPT or H-1B need in a cover letter unprompted
Volunteering visa status in an initial phone screen before being asked

failure_patterns.pattern: Negotiating from personal financial need rather than market data
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students anchor to what they need to survive (student loan payments, living costs) rather than what the market pays, because personal need feels more concrete and urgent
failure_patterns.consequence: Reveals the negotiating floor to the employer, who can then anchor just above it — eliminating all upside from the range
warning_signs: Framing a counter as 'I need at least X because of my loans'
Not having looked up compensation data on levels.fyi or H-1B disclosure databases before negotiating

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting 'we'll figure out sponsorship' as a sufficient commitment before signing
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are relieved to hear a positive sponsorship signal and don't want to seem demanding by pressing for specifics, especially after salary negotiation has already created perceived friction
failure_patterns.consequence: Onboarding into a company that lacks immigration infrastructure — no counsel, no prior H-1B experience — and facing a failed or delayed H-1B filing with status consequences
warning_signs: No written confirmation of H-1B filing intent in the offer documentation
Company cannot name their immigration attorney when asked

failure_patterns.pattern: Believing H-1B dependency means you cannot credibly imply you might leave
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students overestimate the legal and logistical complexity of H-1B transfer and underestimate their rights under portability rules, making employer leverage feel absolute when it is not
failure_patterns.consequence: Surrenders all implied BATNA power in negotiations, allowing employers to make take-it-or-leave-it offers without credible pushback
warning_signs: Not researching H-1B portability rules before entering a negotiation
Declining to explore competing offers because 'it's too complicated with my visa'

failure_patterns.pattern: Conflating sponsorship willingness with sponsorship execution capacity
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Both are binary 'yes/no' answers in conversation, making them feel equivalent — students don't know what questions to ask to distinguish intent from capability
failure_patterns.consequence: Accepting an offer from a company that wants to sponsor but cannot execute, resulting in a failed H-1B and potential status loss
warning_signs: Company has never sponsored an H-1B before but says they're willing to start
No current H-1B employees identifiable at the company

failure_patterns.pattern: Naming a specific salary number before the company has made an offer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates interpret the question as a necessary formality or feel that providing a number demonstrates decisiveness and confidence
failure_patterns.consequence: Anchors the negotiation at the candidate's stated number rather than the company's ceiling; if the number is below what the company would have offered, the candidate has permanently reduced their own outcome
warning_signs: Feeling relieved to 'get the salary question out of the way' early
Treating the compensation question as a qualification screening step rather than a negotiation move

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting an offer on the spot during the verbal delivery call
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Excitement and relief override deliberate decision-making; candidates fear appearing ungrateful or difficult, and recruiters often imply that immediate acceptance is the expected response
failure_patterns.consequence: Signals that the candidate did not know negotiation was possible or expected; leaves money on the table across all negotiable dimensions without any attempt to improve terms
warning_signs: Saying 'Yes, absolutely!' before even hearing the full offer details
Not asking for the offer in writing before committing

failure_patterns.pattern: Revealing current compensation when asked without redirecting
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates assume the question is standard and that refusing to answer will harm their candidacy; the social pressure to answer direct questions overrides strategic awareness
failure_patterns.consequence: Provides the company an anchor for your offer based on your past underpayment rather than your market value; particularly costly for candidates currently earning below market
warning_signs: Treating HR's request for current salary as a required form field
Not having a prepared deflection response before the first recruiter call

failure_patterns.pattern: Negotiating with visible frustration or declining enthusiasm when numbers disappoint
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Genuine emotional reaction to a low offer; candidates conflate 'negotiating hard' with 'expressing disappointment' as a pressure tactic
failure_patterns.consequence: Makes the candidate less attractive at precisely the moment leverage is most needed; companies de-prioritize candidates who seem likely to accept unhappily or leave quickly
warning_signs: Responding to a low offer with a long silence followed by a flat tone
Statements like 'I was hoping for significantly more than that'

failure_patterns.pattern: Letting an exploding offer deadline lapse without explicitly requesting an extension
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Conflict-averse candidates assume that the company will proactively reach out or that their interest is self-evident; they also fear that challenging the deadline will signal wavering commitment
failure_patterns.consequence: Offer lapses; the candidate loses the option entirely when a single direct sentence would have extended the window in nearly every case
warning_signs: Thinking 'they must know I'm still interested' without having communicated it
Treating the deadline as a genuine signal about the company's culture rather than a standard pressure tactic

failure_patterns.pattern: Negotiating solely on salary and stopping when salary hits a ceiling
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates frame compensation mentally as 'salary' rather than 'total package'; when one number stops moving, they conclude negotiation is over
failure_patterns.consequence: Misses signing bonuses, equity grants, professional development budgets, extra vacation, flexible work arrangements — all of which the company may grant freely once salary is exhausted
warning_signs: Accepting 'our salary band is fixed' as the end of the conversation
Not having prepared a list of non-salary items before entering negotiation

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating negotiation as morally questionable or as 'haggling'
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Cultural conditioning, particularly among engineers, frames compensation discussion as unseemly; candidates internalize this as a virtue signal about being 'not money-motivated'
failure_patterns.consequence: Creates systematic and permanent undercompensation that compounds across raises, future offers, and decades of career earnings
warning_signs: Saying 'I don't want to seem greedy' when considering whether to counter
Feeling genuine discomfort or guilt when the recruiter pushes back on a counter-offer

failure_patterns.pattern: Using personal financial need rather than market data as the primary justification for a higher number
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates know their own expenses intimately and assume the employer will find them sympathetic; it feels honest and direct to explain personal circumstances
failure_patterns.consequence: Need-based justifications are weak because they're not connected to the candidate's labor value; they can also signal financial desperation, weakening leverage
warning_signs: Opening a counter with 'I have significant student loans' as the primary argument
Not having researched market compensation data before the negotiation call

failure_patterns.pattern: Comparing offers by base salary only
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Base salary is the most visible and legible number; equity and bonus require calculation and ask-specific questions to quantify, so candidates default to the easily comparable figure
failure_patterns.consequence: Candidates accept offers that are $50,000-$100,000+ lower in actual annual compensation because the base salary appeared competitive
warning_signs: Saying 'both offers are about the same' without calculating annualized equity
Not knowing the bonus target percentage of either offer

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting startup equity valuations from recruiters at face value
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Recruiters present equity with optimistic future valuations that feel compelling; candidates lack the framework to apply stage-appropriate discounts
failure_patterns.consequence: Financial planning built on equity that never materializes, leading to compensation that is effectively just the base salary with no upside realized
warning_signs: Recruiter describes equity value using projected post-IPO or next-round valuations
Candidate cannot answer what the current 409A valuation is

failure_patterns.pattern: Giving a number or accepting verbally on the first offer call
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates feel social pressure to respond positively in the moment; excitement or relief at receiving an offer overrides strategic patience
failure_patterns.consequence: Forfeits negotiating leverage before any counter has been made; the recruiter registers the candidate as satisfied at the initial number
warning_signs: Saying 'that sounds great' or 'I think I can work with that' on the first call
Not knowing what questions to ask about the offer components in the moment

failure_patterns.pattern: Fabricating or exaggerating a competing offer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates know competing offers are powerful leverage but don't have one; they inflate a real offer or invent one to gain negotiating power
failure_patterns.consequence: If the recruiter asks for documentation or the fabrication is discovered, trust is destroyed and the offer may be rescinded; the tactic is explicitly flagged as something never to do
warning_signs: Citing a competing offer from a company whose process hasn't produced a written offer
Being unable to provide specifics when the recruiter asks follow-up questions about the competing offer

failure_patterns.pattern: Using Glassdoor or PayScale for tech compensation research
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: These platforms are widely known brand names for salary data; candidates don't realize they systematically mishandle equity and underreport tech compensation
failure_patterns.consequence: Candidates anchor their negotiation targets to artificially low benchmarks, leaving significant compensation on the table
warning_signs: Citing a salary range from Glassdoor that is 20-30% below levels.fyi data for the same role
Not knowing whether the cited salary figure includes equity

failure_patterns.pattern: Using the final commitment close ('I'll sign today if you reach X') too early or insincerely
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The tactic sounds powerful and candidates deploy it as a negotiating probe rather than a genuine commitment
failure_patterns.consequence: Destroys credibility with the recruiter; if the number is met and the candidate still doesn't sign, the recruiter loses trust and future negotiations are compromised
warning_signs: Making the commitment close before having fully evaluated the offer
Using the phrase more than once in the same negotiation

diagnostic_questions.question: Has this company sponsored H-1B before, and what is their historical approval rate?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the offer is executable on immigration — a company with no prior H-1B history or low approval rates is a fundamentally different risk than one with a track record
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Who is their immigration counsel, and can you get written confirmation of sponsorship intent in the offer?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Do they have immigration counsel retained? Are they willing to commit in writing? If neither, treat the immigration component of the offer as unconfirmed.

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your OPT end date, and when is the next H-1B cap filing window?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: How much time pressure you're actually under — which determines whether you have runway to run a multi-company search or whether you're in a deadline-driven single-offer scenario
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If you have 6+ months, you have time for parallel processes; treat this like any other negotiation with competing offers as the goal.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If you're within 90 days of OPT expiration, identify your real BATNA — STEM OPT extension, another degree, CPT, or another country's job market — before entering any negotiation.

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your real BATNA — what would you actually do if this offer fell through?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether your negotiating leverage is genuine or performed — a BATNA only functions if you would credibly pursue it; an unconvincing alternative gives the employer all the power
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If you have a competing offer or credible alternative, your leverage is real — use it explicitly and anchor high.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If your BATNA is weak, invest in building one: run parallel processes, identify extension options, or explore other markets before negotiating.

diagnostic_questions.question: What OEWS wage level would the offered salary classify you at under the post-2025 H-1B lottery system?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether there is an alignment between salary negotiation and immigration outcome — a Level I or II offer may be negotiable upward using the mutual lottery odds benefit as leverage
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If Level III or IV, confirm this in your negotiation framing as a shared benefit. If Level I or II, use the wage-weighted lottery argument explicitly to push for Level III classification.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If the company is unaware of the wage-weighted lottery system, educating them may itself open a salary conversation that wasn't available before.

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you anchoring your counter to market data, or to what feels safe given your visa situation?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you are negotiating as a rational market participant or as a fear-managed applicant — the answer determines whether your counter has external validity or just reflects anxiety
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If anchored to market data (levels.fyi, H-1B disclosures), your counter is defensible and credible — proceed with confidence.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If anchored to personal comfort or fear, stop and research market comps for your role, level, and company before sending any counter.

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you have written confirmation of H-1B sponsorship intent as part of the offer package?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the immigration component of the offer is real or informal — verbal commitments are unenforceable and leave you exposed if the company's circumstances or personnel change
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm it specifies H-1B filing (not just OPT extension), the expected timeline, and names the immigration attorney or firm.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Do not sign until you have written confirmation — request it explicitly as a condition of acceptance and treat the absence of it as a yellow flag about execution capacity.

diagnostic_questions.question: Does the company know you're aware of H-1B portability rights and that you have real alternatives?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you have implicitly ceded BATNA power by signaling lock-in — employers who believe you cannot leave will price comp accordingly, even if the legal reality is more flexible
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If you've signaled awareness of portability and alternatives, you're negotiating from a stronger position than most visa holders in the same situation.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Consider whether your communication has inadvertently signaled desperation or lock-in — adjust framing to imply optionality without being adversarial.

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you received a formal or informal signal that the company wants to hire you — or are you still in evaluation?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you are in 'Yes-If' (post-hiring-decision) or 'No-But' (still being evaluated) status, which determines how much leverage you actually have
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What specific comp dimensions have they discussed — salary only, or total package including bonus and equity?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Defer all compensation discussion until you receive an offer signal; negotiating during evaluation forces premature compromise

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you have any competing offers, or imminent offers from companies you would actually work for?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you have real market leverage or are negotiating purely on assertion; competing offers are the most powerful single source of negotiating power
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Do the offer windows actually overlap — is the competing offer still active right now?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Are you actively interviewing elsewhere, and can you compress timelines to create overlap? If not, your leverage must come from other sources — quantified value propositions, market data, or demonstrated alternatives

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you disclosed your current salary or named a specific salary number at any point in this process?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether information asymmetry has already been compromised; if you've named a number, the negotiation is partially anchored and strategy must shift
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Was the number you named above or below what they've offered? If below, you need to reframe using market data rather than trying to walk back your stated floor
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Good — maintain that asymmetry; prepare deflection scripts for when they ask again

diagnostic_questions.question: When the company pushed back on your counter, what did you say and what was your tone?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is maintaining positive framing under pressure or letting frustration show; enthusiasm loss during negotiation is one of the most common and costly mistakes
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Did you reiterate excitement for the role before restating your ask, or did you lead with the counter?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Have you countered yet at all, or accepted the pushback as final?

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you negotiating only on base salary, or have you mapped out all the dimensions you could adjust?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate understands that negotiation is multi-dimensional and whether they know where company flexibility actually lives
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Which non-salary items have you already asked about — signing bonus, equity cliff, vacation, remote work, professional development budget?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Why not — do you believe those items aren't negotiable, or did salary seem like the highest-value target?

diagnostic_questions.question: Does this offer come with a deadline, and have you explicitly addressed it with the recruiter?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether there is an exploding offer dynamic in play, and whether the candidate knows they must explicitly request an extension rather than assuming it will be granted
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you stated clearly that the timeline doesn't work for you and asked for an extension?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: How much time do you have? Is this enough to run your decision process deliberately, or are you being implicitly pressured?

diagnostic_questions.question: What justification have you given or are you planning to give for your counter-request?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is using market-based framing (strong) or personal-need framing (weak), and whether they have a specific reason at all
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Is the reason tied to market data, your quantified impact, or personal circumstance? The first two are significantly more persuasive
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: You need a reason — requests without justification feel arbitrary; even a modest reason substantially increases acceptance rate

diagnostic_questions.question: How many interview rounds has this company put you through, and how many people have they involved?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: The company's sunk investment in your candidacy; more rounds and more interviewers means stronger pressure on their side to reach agreement, which is leverage you can use
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you internalized that they are highly motivated to close you — or are you still treating yourself as the supplicant in this transaction?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Are you early in the process? If so, focus on establishing fit before any comp discussion

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you calculated the full annual total compensation for each offer, including annualized equity, target bonus, and base — excluding the signing bonus?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is comparing offers on a valid apples-to-apples basis or defaulting to base salary comparison
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What is the annualized equity value and what discount rate did you apply based on company stage?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The offer ranking may be reversed once full comp is modeled; what is the equity grant, vesting schedule, and bonus target for each offer?

diagnostic_questions.question: What stage is the company — public, pre-IPO Series C/D, or early-stage Seed/A?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Which equity valuation framework applies and how much weight to give the equity component in financial planning
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: For pre-IPO: have you asked for the 409A valuation and the liquidation preference structure?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If company stage is unknown, the candidate cannot accurately value the equity or assess liquidity risk

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you have a competing offer from a company you would genuinely consider working for?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the most powerful negotiation lever is available and can be used credibly
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you mentioned it to the recruiter, and have you framed your target company as the first choice?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Are there other processes in flight that could produce a competing offer before the current deadline?

diagnostic_questions.question: What level is the offer, and have you confirmed whether it is at the low, mid, or high end of that band?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate is evaluating near-term comp or long-term career trajectory; a misleveled offer has compounding consequences
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Does the offered level match the scope and expectations described in the role?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The candidate cannot accurately benchmark the offer or assess career progression potential without this information

diagnostic_questions.question: For option grants specifically: what is the exercise window after leaving the company?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the equity creates a golden handcuff that limits the candidate's future job mobility
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If 90 days: can you afford to exercise vested options out of pocket if you leave before a liquidity event?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Candidate may be agreeing to equity terms that trap them financially without realizing it

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you checked levels.fyi for the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentile total comp for your specific level and role at this company?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the candidate has objective market data to anchor their counter-offer or is negotiating without a benchmark
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Where does the current offer fall in that range, and what percentile are you targeting?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Candidate is likely to either anchor too low or make an ask that is not credibly supported by data

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you genuinely willing to walk away from this offer if the company does not meet your target number?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the negotiation is real or performative; the source states that the final close and firm counter only work if the candidate is actually willing to decline
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Then name a specific total comp target and hold to it through the negotiation
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Adjust negotiation tactics accordingly; do not use the commitment close and do not make ultimatums you will not follow through on

industry_realities.common_belief: Sponsoring H-1B is expensive and justifies paying an international candidate below market
industry_realities.actual_reality: H-1B sponsorship costs $3,000–$8,000/year in attorney and USCIS fees — less than 5% of a $150,000+ employee's total cost. It is not a meaningful financial justification for 20% below-market compensation.
industry_realities.implication: When an employer uses sponsorship cost as a reason to offer less, it is a negotiation tactic, not a financial reality. Counter with the actual cost figures.

industry_realities.common_belief: Pushing back on an offer as an international student risks having it rescinded, especially with sponsorship at stake
industry_realities.actual_reality: Offers are almost never rescinded for reasonable negotiation — companies have invested too much in the hiring process to withdraw over a counter. The fear of rescission is systematically miscalibrated.
industry_realities.implication: International students should negotiate with the same assertiveness as domestic candidates. The sponsorship relationship is not made fragile by a salary counter; it is made fragile by poor performance or bad cultural fit.

industry_realities.common_belief: H-1B holders are legally locked in to their employer and cannot credibly threaten to leave
industry_realities.actual_reality: H-1B portability rules allow you to switch employers without waiting for approval, as long as the new petition is filed within 60 days of the switch. You are not trapped.
industry_realities.implication: Knowing and internalizing portability rights converts a perceived permanent constraint into a manageable one — and makes competing offer leverage genuinely credible for H-1B holders.

industry_realities.common_belief: Visa status is purely a negotiating liability — it makes you a more expensive, riskier hire
industry_realities.actual_reality: Visa holders have a demonstrable retention advantage — they are significantly less likely to job-hop due to immigration switching costs, meaning employers get a longer-tenure employee for the same investment
industry_realities.implication: Visa holders should explicitly surface retention value as a counter-narrative to the cost framing, reorienting the conversation from 'I cost more because of sponsorship' to 'I stay longer and that has real value.'

industry_realities.common_belief: If a company says they will sponsor H-1B, the immigration component of the offer is settled
industry_realities.actual_reality: Sponsorship willingness and execution capacity are entirely different things. A company without immigration counsel, prior H-1B filings, or current H-1B employees may be genuinely willing but structurally unable to deliver.
industry_realities.implication: Verify execution infrastructure — not just intent — before signing. Ask about immigration counsel, prior H-1B history, and current H-1B employees as due diligence items equivalent to verifying salary.

industry_realities.common_belief: Under the H-1B lottery, salary negotiation and immigration strategy are separate concerns
industry_realities.actual_reality: Under the post-2025 wage-weighted lottery, negotiating for a higher wage level directly improves lottery odds — salary negotiation and immigration outcomes are structurally linked
industry_realities.implication: Frame salary negotiations in the context of wage level classification explicitly — a higher comp ask is simultaneously an immigration strategy, and both parties benefit from a Level III or IV classification.

industry_realities.common_belief: You need a competing job offer to have real negotiating leverage
industry_realities.actual_reality: A BATNA can be any credible alternative you would genuinely pursue — another degree (resetting OPT clock), CPT positions, or another country's job market. It does not have to be a competing offer.
industry_realities.implication: International students who believe they have no leverage because they lack a competing offer may still have real alternatives they haven't articulated. Identify and document your actual BATNA before entering any negotiation.

industry_realities.common_belief: Negotiating a job offer is rude, risky, or signals that you only care about money
industry_realities.actual_reality: Experienced hiring managers expect negotiation; candidates who accept immediately are often perceived as either inexperienced or unaware of their market value — both of which are competence signals that work against you
industry_realities.implication: Always counter at least once, framed positively; negotiation signals professional sophistication, not greed

industry_realities.common_belief: A salary increase of a few thousand dollars isn't worth the awkwardness of negotiating
industry_realities.actual_reality: A $5,000 salary increase compounded over 10 years is worth approximately $100,000 gross; it also sets the baseline for every future raise and the anchor for your next employer's offer
industry_realities.implication: Calculate the true long-term value before deciding a request is too small to make; the compounding effect makes early-career negotiations disproportionately important

industry_realities.common_belief: The company is doing you a favor by hiring you
industry_realities.actual_reality: By the time an offer is extended, the company has already invested tens of thousands of dollars in interviewing you and has decided they need you to solve a specific business problem; they are highly motivated not to lose you
industry_realities.implication: Enter negotiation understanding that you have substantial leverage — the company wants to close this deal as much as you do

industry_realities.common_belief: If you ask for more than they can give, they'll rescind the offer
industry_realities.actual_reality: The absolute worst realistic outcome of a reasonable counter-offer is receiving the original offer unchanged; companies do not rescind offers because candidates negotiated in good faith
industry_realities.implication: The asymmetry of outcomes (upside: more money; downside: original offer) makes negotiating always the correct expected-value decision

industry_realities.common_belief: The salary the company offers reflects what the market says you're worth
industry_realities.actual_reality: First offers reflect the company's budget target and what they think they can get away with based on information they have about you; it is not a market clearing price
industry_realities.implication: Treat the first offer as the opening position in a negotiation, not as a take-it-or-leave-it assessment of your value

industry_realities.common_belief: Exploding offer deadlines are genuine signals that the company needs a fast decision
industry_realities.actual_reality: Exploding offers are pressure tactics; virtually every one extends when the candidate explicitly calls it out and requests more time
industry_realities.implication: Never let an exploding offer deadline lapse silently; always request an extension explicitly and frame it around making an informed, committed decision

industry_realities.common_belief: Salary is the main thing you can negotiate, and once it's fixed, negotiation is over
industry_realities.actual_reality: Companies often have far more flexibility on signing bonuses, equity, vacation days, professional development budgets, remote work, and project assignment than on base salary, which is constrained by pay bands and internal equity concerns
industry_realities.implication: When salary hits a ceiling, pivot immediately to other dimensions rather than accepting the ceiling as the end of the conversation

industry_realities.common_belief: Having a competing offer is a form of bluffing or manipulation
industry_realities.actual_reality: Companies rationally respond to competing offers because a single interview is a weak signal of capability; multiple companies making offers is a much stronger combined signal — demand breeds demand
industry_realities.implication: Generating real competing offers through a serious parallel job search is both ethical and strategically essential; it is the most reliable lever available

industry_realities.common_belief: Providing your current salary when asked is a routine step that doesn't affect the outcome
industry_realities.actual_reality: Your current salary becomes the anchor for their offer; a company that knows you earn $70K will offer $75–80K even if they budgeted $95K, because your disclosure has justified underpaying you relative to market
industry_realities.implication: Deflect current salary questions using prepared scripts; the information asymmetry created by not disclosing is directly worth thousands of dollars

industry_realities.common_belief: A small salary increase is too trivial for a company to care about
industry_realities.actual_reality: Employers view employees at 150–200% loaded cost; they think about headcount budget in aggregate, not individual salaries; a $5K salary increase in their budget view is literally 'mouse droppings'
industry_realities.implication: Don't be embarrassed to make requests that seem large to you but are proportionally tiny in the company's budget context

industry_realities.common_belief: Glassdoor and PayScale give you accurate salary benchmarks for tech roles
industry_realities.actual_reality: These platforms wildly mishandle equity compensation and systematically underreport total comp for tech; levels.fyi and H-1B filings are the authoritative sources for tech compensation
industry_realities.implication: Candidates who benchmark using Glassdoor anchor their negotiations too low and leave significant money on the table; switch to levels.fyi before any negotiation

industry_realities.common_belief: Salary is the most important and negotiable component of an offer
industry_realities.actual_reality: Equity is often 30-60% of total comp at large tech companies; signing bonuses are one-time and the easiest component for companies to approve; base salary is often the most constrained by band limits
industry_realities.implication: When base is capped, shift negotiation focus to signing bonus and equity refresh rather than continuing to push on base

industry_realities.common_belief: Recruiters will volunteer all relevant compensation details when making an offer
industry_realities.actual_reality: Critical details like the option exercise window, fully diluted ownership percentage, liquidation preferences, and 409A valuations are almost never volunteered; they must be explicitly asked for
industry_realities.implication: Candidates must run a structured checklist of equity questions on every offer call or risk agreeing to terms that significantly reduce actual compensation value

industry_realities.common_belief: Asking for more will make you seem greedy and risk the offer being rescinded
industry_realities.actual_reality: People who negotiate get an average 18.83% more than those who accept the first offer; offers are almost never rescinded for polite, well-framed negotiation
industry_realities.implication: The expected value of negotiating is highly positive and the downside risk is near zero when done with warmth and professionalism

industry_realities.common_belief: A competing offer from a company you wouldn't actually take is just as useful as a real one
industry_realities.actual_reality: Fabricating or exaggerating competing offers is explicitly flagged as something to never do; the tactic only works when genuine and can destroy the offer if discovered
industry_realities.implication: Only cite competing offers that are real, documented, and from companies you would actually consider; the leverage comes from credibility, not the existence of a number

industry_realities.common_belief: The equity value the recruiter quotes is what the equity is worth
industry_realities.actual_reality: Recruiter-quoted equity valuations for pre-IPO companies are based on hoped-for future valuations, not current 409A values; early-stage equity should be valued at $0 for budgeting
industry_realities.implication: Apply stage-appropriate discount rates to all equity: public companies at 80-90% of market value, Series C-D at 60-90%, early-stage at $0 for planning purposes

industry_realities.common_belief: Signing bonuses are part of your ongoing compensation
industry_realities.actual_reality: Signing bonuses are one-time payments; your year-2 and beyond compensation is materially lower unless the bonus is renewed, which is not standard
industry_realities.implication: Always model annual recurring compensation without the signing bonus to understand the true ongoing offer value; signing bonus is a bridge, not a salary component

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Response to receiving the first offer
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Accepts immediately or counter-proposes a number based on personal need or what feels safe, driven by relief that the offer exists and fear that any friction will cost the sponsorship
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats the first offer as an opening position, researches market comps before responding, and counters with a specific number grounded in levels.fyi and H-1B salary disclosure data
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The difference between accepting and countering compounds to 10–30% salary differential annually, and hundreds of thousands of dollars over a career

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Framing of visa status in negotiations
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats visa dependency as a liability to manage or hide — either over-apologizes for needing sponsorship or stays silent about immigration topics to avoid attention
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Proactively reframes visa status as a retention differentiator and uses it as a negotiating asset — 'I stay longer, which means your investment in sponsoring me has a higher ROI'
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Framing determines whether the employer sees sponsorship as a cost to minimize or an investment in a long-tenure employee — the same fact generates opposite leverage depending on who frames it first

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Discloses OPT/sponsorship need early — in cover letters, early applications, or first contact — out of a sense of obligation to be upfront
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Delays disclosure until the company has decided they want to hire, then states it matter-of-factly as a researched, confident choice — 'I've confirmed this company sponsors H-1B, which is part of why I applied'
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Early disclosure makes sponsorship cost a filter before qualifications are assessed; delayed disclosure ensures the employer weighs your value before weighing the cost

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Believes H-1B dependency is a near-total lock-in — that switching employers is so complex and risky that it is not a real option, eliminating all implied BATNA credibility
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Knows H-1B portability rules precisely — 60-day filing window, ability to start immediately — and uses this knowledge to maintain credible optionality in negotiations
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Employers price compensation partly based on perceived switching costs; a candidate who knows they can leave commands more leverage than one who believes they cannot

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats any 'yes we'll sponsor' signal as confirmation that the immigration component is settled, and moves on to evaluating only the salary number
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Distinguishes sponsorship willingness from execution capacity — asks about immigration counsel, prior H-1B history, current H-1B employees, and gets written confirmation before signing
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A company that wants to sponsor but cannot execute is a material risk that looks identical to a capable sponsor in early conversation — only due diligence reveals the difference

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Believes BATNA requires a competing job offer and, lacking one, concludes they have no leverage and must accept whatever is offered
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Recognizes that any credible alternative counts — another degree, CPT, another country's market — and actively builds and articulates BATNA options before entering negotiation
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Perceived optionality determines negotiating posture; a student who identifies real alternatives negotiates with authentic confidence rather than performed confidence

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Integration of immigration and compensation strategy
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats salary negotiation and immigration planning as separate tracks — negotiates comp with HR and handles visa logistics separately with no connection between the two
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Understands that post-2025 wage level classification directly affects H-1B lottery odds and explicitly uses this alignment to make a joint case for higher comp and better immigration outcomes
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Using the wage-weighted lottery argument converts a one-sided salary ask into a mutual benefit conversation — structurally more persuasive and less adversarial

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats the offer as a gift or favor from the employer; feels grateful and reluctant to 'push back' on a company that has chosen them
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats the offer as a mutual market transaction; understands the company has a problem they need solved and has already invested heavily to find the right person
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The mental model determines the entire negotiation posture; gratitude framing produces deference, while transaction framing produces calibrated confidence that recruiters read as competence

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Accepts on the spot or asks for time to think before accepting without countering
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Always counters at least once across at least one dimension before accepting, treating the counter as a standard and expected step
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: First offers are opening positions, not final offers; experienced professionals know this and act accordingly — leaving the first offer unchanged signals either naivety or lack of self-advocacy

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Answers recruiter questions directly and honestly, including current salary and specific salary expectations, treating transparency as a virtue
